 2 
INTRODUCTION 
1. This matter concerns the constitutionality of a national policy that prohibits, 
without exception, all incarcerated persons who are registered for tertiary 
academic courses from using personal laptops for their studies in prison cells.  
2. The respondent, Mbalenhle Sydney Ntuli (“Mr Ntuli”), is an inmate of the 
Department of Correctional Services. He has been undertaking tertiary distance 
learning at his family’s personal expense.1 After initially being permitted the use 
of a personal laptop in his cell, Mr Ntuli was moved to a new facility and prohibited 
from using his laptop in his cell for study purposes.  
3. After having been denied permission to use his personal computer in his single 
cell for study purposes, Mr Ntuli successfully challenged the Correctional 
Services Policy Procedures on Further Education adopted by the Acting 
National Commissioner of Correctional Services in 2007 (“the Policy”) before 
the High Court, Gauteng Local Division, Johannesburg. Matsemela AJ found the 
policy’s prohibition which states that “no computer shall be allowed in any cell 
(communal and/or single)”2 to be inconsistent with the Constitution and unlawful.3  
4. Mr Ntuli was successful in the High Court.4 Matsemela AJ held: 
4.1. the Policy constituted unfair discrimination in terms of the Promotion of 
Equality and Prevention of Unfair Discrimination Act 4 of 2000 (“the 
Equality Act”) as against the respondent; 
4.2. the Policy unjustifiably limited the right to further education of all inmates 
 
1  Vol 1, Founding Affidavit para 11 p 8. 
2  Vol 1, Annexure “MSN2” Policy Procedures Directorate Formal Education dated 08 February 
2007, p 25 (item two, lines 7 – 9). 
3  Vol 6, High Court Order, para 2, p 525. 
4  Vol 6, High Court Judgment, p 512; the Order appears from p 525. 
 3 
and was inconsistent with the Constitution. Within this order Matsemela 
AJ erroneously referred to paragraph numbers of a previous policy; and 
4.3. Mr Ntuli was granted the right to use his personal computer without the 
use of a modem in his cell, for so long as he remains a registered student 
with any recognised tertiary institution in South Africa and he is required 
to make his computer available for inspection at any given time by any 
representative of the applicants.  
5. The applicants were granted leave to appeal to the Supreme Court of Appeal 
(“SCA”).  
6. The SCA dismissed the appeal but varied the terms of the High Court’s order, 
ordering as follows: 
“3 The appeal is partially upheld and the order of the court a quo is set aside 
and replaced with the following: 
‘1. To the extent that the Policy Procedure Directorate Formal 
Education as approved by the second respondent and dated 8 February 
2007 prohibits the use of personal computers in cells, it is declared 
invalid and set aside.  
2. The order in paragraph 1 is suspended for 12 months from the 
date of this order. 
3. The first and second respondents are directed, within 12 months 
from the date of this order, after consultation with the Judicial 
Inspectorate for Correctional Services (“JICS”), to prepare and 
promulgate a revised policy for correctional centres permitting the use of 
personal computers in cells for study purposes (“the revised policy”). 
4. The first and second respondents are directed, within one week 
after promulgating the revised policy, to disseminate that policy to the 
head of every correctional centre, and, where one is employed, to the 
head of education at each centre. 
5. Notice of the revised policy must be posted on notice boards in all 
prisons where prisoners customarily receive information, and such notice 
must set out where prisoners may obtain copies of the revised policy.  
 4 
6. Pending the revision of the education policy:  
6.1 The applicant is entitled to use his personal computer in his cell, 
without the use of a modem, for as long as he remains a registered 
student with a recognised tertiary or further education institution in South 
Africa. 
6.2 Any registered student in a correctional centre who needs a 
computer to support their studies, and/or any student who has registered 
for a course of study that requires a computer as a compulsory part of 
the course, is entitled to use their personal computer without the use of 
a modem in their cell for as long as they remain a registered student with 
a recognised tertiary or further education institution in South Africa. 
6.3 The applicant or any other student who keeps a personal 
computer in their cell in accordance with paragraphs 6.1 and 6.2 above 
must make it available for inspection at any given time by the head of the 
correctional centre or any representative of the first and second 
respondents. 
6.4 In the event of a breach of the rules relating to the use by a 
prisoner of their computer in their cell, the head of the correctional 
services centre may, after considering any representations the prisoner 
may make, direct that the prisoner may not use their computer in their 
cell.  
7. The first and second respondents are to pay the cost of this 
application jointly and severally, the one paying the other to be absolved.’ 
4 The first and second appellants are to pay, jointly and severally, the costs 
of the application for leave to appeal before the high court.  
5 The first and second appellants are to pay, jointly and severally, the costs 
of the appeal, including the costs of two counsel.  
6 The first and second appellants are directed to disseminate this 
order to all correctional centres and make it available to prisoners, within 
ten days of the order.”5 
 
7. The SCA order accordingly granted interim relief permitting Mr Ntuli and any other 
registered student who needs a computer to use their personal computer in their 
cell, subject to making it available for any inspection whenever requested, and 
 
5  Vol 6, SCA Order, pp 491 – 493. 
 5 
permitting the head of a facility to prohibit use in the event of a breach of rules. 
Mr Ntuli has accordingly been using his personal laptop for study purposes, as 
he did previously after the High Court judgment and – before that – when he was 
initially in the Medium B facility which had no computer room. There are many 
other prisoners who have similarly been using their personal computers for study 
during this litigation, such as the applicants in Pretorius who had use of their 
laptops in cells for 11 years until 2017.6  
8. The applicants seek to enforce the absolute ban in the Policy and prevent any 
prisoners from using personal laptops for study in their cells under any 
circumstances.  
9. The second and third applicants, the National Commissioner of Correctional 
Services and the Head of Correctional Centre, Johannesburg Medium C 
(collectively, “the applicants”) therefore apply for leave to appeal against the 
judgment and order handed down by the SCA. 
10. The Judicial Inspectorate for Correctional Services (“JICS”), an independent 
statutory body established in terms of section 85 of the Correctional Services Act 
111 of 1998, applied for leave to be admitted as an amicus curiae before the 
SCA.7 JICS acts under the control of the Inspecting Judge, currently Justice 
Edwin Cameron, and is mandated, in terms of section 85(2) of the Correctional 
Services Act, to inspect and monitor all correctional facilities managed by the 
Department to ensure that incarcerated persons’ constitutional rights are not 
violated.8 Before the SCA, JICS submitted that the Policy is inconsistent with 
 
6  Pretorius and Others v Minister of Justice and Correctional Services and Others [2018] ZAGPPHC 
347; 2018 (2) SACR 501 (GP) at para 21.  
7  Vol 5, JICS’ Notice of Motion, p 430.  
8  Vol 5, JICS’ Founding Affidavit, paras 16 – 18, p 437. 
 6 
domestic and international law.9 The SCA ordered the Minister and National 
Commissioner to consult with JICS on the revision of the Policy.10  
11. The respondent concedes that this matter raises constitutional issues of general 
public importance but submits that the application for leave to appeal ought not 
to be granted. In what follows we will address: 
11.1. The Policy at issue; 
11.2. Mr Ntuli’s position as a student inmate; 
11.3. The constitutional rights of prisoners and the ‘residuum principle’; 
11.4. The specific rights undermined by the Policy; 
11.5. The alleged security risks posed; and 
11.6. The appropriate deference afforded to the separation of powers in the 
SCA’s Order. 
THE POLICY  
12. The Policy was approved by the Acting Commissioner of Correctional Services 
in January 2007.11 
13. The Policy regulates the use of computers in a variety of ways. Some of the 
restrictions are as follows:  
“Only registered students (offenders) who have a need for a computer as 
supportive to his/her studies, and / or offenders who have registered for 
a study field / course that requires a computer as compulsory party of the 
 
9  Vol 5, JICS’ Founding Affidavit, para 32, p 440. 
10  Vol 6, SCA Order, para 3.3, p 491. 
11  Vol 1, Annexure “AA1”: Policy Procedures: Formal Education Programme dated 08 February 
2007, pp 46 – 54. 
 7 
course are allowed to have a personal computer within the Correctional 
Facility.”  
“All applications relating to the utilization of personal computers received 
from offenders must be approved by the Head of the Correctional 
Centre.”12  
“Ensure that offenders have no access to the internet through the use of 
a computer, computer system, network, communication service provider 
or remote computing service. Violation of this procedure may result in 
disciplinary actions.”13  
“Ensure that computers are ‘secure’ at all times and will have no negative 
effect on the security and operations within the Correctional Centre as 
well as the safety of officials and other offenders. After completion of the 
registered course the personal computer should be removed from the 
centre immediately.”14  
14. The Policy therefore provides a mechanism for the Correctional Centres to keep 
computer usage in check and ensure it does not negatively affect the security, 
operations and safety. 
15. The Policy’s central purpose is set out as follows: 
“The objective of education programmes is to utilize education as a basis 
for further development opportunities for offenders. These programmes 
should be outcomes-based, needs driven, cost effective and market-
related in order to empower offenders for sustainable life after release.”15 
16. Despite the underlying purpose of the Policy to facilitate education and access to 
reading material, it contains the following prohibition, “no computer shall be 
allowed in any cell (communal and/or single)”.  
17. The Policy prohibits all students like Mr Ntuli from using personal computers 
within their cells. It is a blanket prohibition with no exceptions. Students are only 
permitted to use their computers within a communal computer room at the 
 
12  Vol 1, Annexure “AA1” p 50. 
13  Vol 1, Annexure “AA1” p 51. 
14  Vol 1, Annexure “AA1” p 52. 
15  Vol 1, Annexure “AA1” p 47. 
 8 
correctional centre for limited hours from Monday to Friday. Not all correctional 
facilities have communal computer rooms. 
MR NTULI’S POSITION 
Studying on a personal computer 
18. A computer has been instrumental for Mr Ntuli’s studies.16 Previously, he had 
permission to use a computer in his single cell when registered as a student while 
an inmate of Johannesburg Medium B Correctional Centre. It was removed when 
Mr Ntuli was moved to Johannesburg Medium C Correctional Centre.17  
19. At the time the application was brought, Mr Ntuli was studying computer studies 
focused on data processing with the Oxbridge Academy. Mr Ntuli continues to 
undertake distance studies. His studies are at his family’s expense.18  
20. For inmates such as Mr Ntuli, a computer is not just the tool for studying but is in 
fact the object of study. It is impossible to study through a different medium. As 
Mr Ntuli states in his founding papers, “I need the use of a personal computer in 
order to complete this course, since it is absolutely absurd that a course in data 
processing can be done on paper.”19 There is no substantive response to this 
from the applicants, but simply a bare denial.20  
21. Mr Ntuli raised the concern that the Policy on formal education is antiquated and 
does not take into consideration the specific study methods of a student inmate. 
He states that: 
“study methods and accessibility of study material has changed 
 
16  Vol 1, Founding Affidavit, para 11, p 8. 
17  Vol 1, High Court Judgment paras 6 and 7, p 514. 
18  Vol 6 Opposing Affidavit, paras 4 and 5, p 527. 
19  Vol 1, Founding Affidavit para 11 p 8. 
20  Vol 1, Answering Affidavit, para 10.1 p 37. 
 9 
considerably since 2007. Personal computers play an important role as 
a means to access information which was in the past confined to 
textbooks. Personal computers have in many ways replaced 
conventional textbooks and therefore access to a personal computer by 
students has become a necessity and not a privilege.”21  
22. The founding papers identified the need for, and the benefits of, electronic study 
– including lower costs and speed of access:  
“A personal computer is critical to success in my field of study, and 
also the cost of electronic versions of books are much cheaper than 
hardcopies of the same prescribed textbooks. In addition thereto, 
downloading my study materials in electronic format from the student 
website is much faster than receiving it through conventional post 
which is so unreliable that most correspondence colleges strongly 
discourage its registered students from making use of it. It is also far 
more convenient and safer for me to submit my completed 
assignments online.”22  
23. This explanation of the need for electronic study was again simply met with a 
bare denial from the applicants.23  
24. Mr Ntuli referred to the positive effect of prison education programs on recidivism 
and the importance of education in reducing recidivism as espoused by the 
previous Minister of Correctional Services, Mr Sibusiso Ndebele.24 This was, 
correctly, not denied by the applicants.25  
25. In response to the allegations in the founding papers, the applicants either relied 
on bare denials or erroneously claimed that Mr Ntuli could not make such 
assertions since he was not a registered student. The latter claim was withdrawn 
in the supplementary affidavit, since Mr Ntuli had clear evidence of his student 
registration which had been accepted by the Centre.26 It is thus undisputed on 
 
21  Vol 1, Founding Affidavit, para 21, pp 11 – 12. 
22  Vol 1, Founding Affidavit, para 24, p 13. 
23  Vol 1, Answering Affidavit, para 21.1, p 42. 
24  Vol 1, Founding Affidavit, para 22, p 12. 
25  Vol 1, Answering Affidavit, para 19.1, p 42. 
26  Vol 2, Supplementary Affidavit para 4 p 88. 
 10 
the papers that: 
25.1. For student inmates, such as Mr Ntuli, access to their personal computers 
is essential for undertaking their studies; 
25.2. The Policy fails to accommodate the educational needs of students 
studying on and with computers; 
25.3. Access to computers and electronic study provides benefits to students 
through lower costs and speed of access to materials and submissions of 
assignments; and 
25.4.  Education is an important factor in reducing recidivism in inmates. 
26. The SCA found, “[a]s Mr Ntuli’s affidavit makes clear, access to a computer is an 
essential requirement of computer studies. This is not disputed”.27 As recorded 
in the SCA judgment, Mr Ntuli’s computer is both the object of study and the 
means by which that study takes place.28 The SCA further recognised that even 
in courses outside of computer sciences, educational materials are most 
conveniently and economically accessed on a computer and course work is now 
routinely composed and submitted electronically.29 
Access to a computer hub does not justify the prohibition  
27. We submit that the actions taken by the Department to assist in providing access 
to further education are not relevant to the negative obligation not to restrict 
students from effectively pursuing their studies. As the SCA explained: 
 
27  Vol 6, SCA Judgment, para 21, p 501. 
28  Vol 6, SCA Judgment, para 8, p 496. 
29  Vol 6, SCA Judgment, para 23, p 502. 
 11 
“it is no answer to contend, as the appellants do, that adequate provision 
has been made for Mr Ntuli to have access to a computer in the prison’s 
computer centre…The right of a prisoner to pursue further education is 
not determined by what might suffice to pass his chosen course of study. 
Rather, the right is to pursue the course he has chosen. That entails 
using time that is otherwise uncommitted, whilst confined in his cell, to 
study. And to do so in a way that is effective, which in the case of Mr 
Ntuli’s computer course, is with the use of a personal computer.”30  
28. The applicants deny that the prohibition on computers in single cells violates the 
right to education. The applicants rely on the claim that there are systems in place 
to use computers in the computer hub or to apply to use a personal computer in 
the computer hub.31 Further, they claim that “that the right to education is not 
being deprived of infringed, it is purely being regulated in a reasonable manner 
under the circumstances imposed on the offenders.”32  
29. Mr Ntuli submits that the systems in place to do not permit sufficient access to a 
computer and the restrictions on access are neither reasonable nor justifiable.  
The computer centre is open for five hours on weekdays and Mr Ntuli is permitted 
to be outside of his cell daily for approximately six hours in total. In this time, in 
addition to accessing the computer center, he must “consume breakfast and 
lunch, exercise, take a shower, clean [his] cell and do [his] laundry. Since every 
other cell in the entire centre is also unlocked during the same time, the centre is 
exceptionally noisy and studying with many distractions is extremely 
challenging.”33 
30. These time constraints are confirmed by the applicants, who state that Mr Ntuli 
“is a maximum offender as a result his hours as when he is to be permitted in and 
 
30  Vol 6, SCA Judgment, para 22, p 502. 
31  Vol 1, Answering Affidavit, para 13.5, p 39. 
32  Vol 1, Answering Affidavit, para 19.2, p 42. 
33  Vol 1, Founding Affidavit, paras 15 to 17, p 10.  
 12 
out of his cell are restricted.”34 These time constraints and obstacles to study 
were considered and accepted by the SCA.35  
31. It is common cause that inmates, especially maximum offenders, are afforded 
restricted hours outside of their cells. It is in this context that access to personal 
computers in single cells becomes even more necessary. A substantial amount 
of time is spent locked up in cells, which could be constructively utilized for study 
purposes.36 Mr Ntuli explains how this affects him: 
“I am locked in my cell for 17hours and 45 minutes a day. I can only 
reasonably sleep 8-9 hours a night. This effectively means that 
approximately 8 hours a day which I could spend studying are being 
wasted as a result of the extremely restrictive policies of the 
Department of Correctional Services.”37  
32. The applicants simply provide a bare denial regarding Mr Ntuli’s explanation of 
the hours that are available to him daily and the potential study hours that are 
denied him and therefore wasted.38  It cannot be plausibly disputed that access 
to a computer within an inmate’s cell would provide further hours for study.  
33. If an inmate’s available hours for studying outside their cells are limited and many 
hours could be utilised for studying while they are locked up for the majority of 
the day, why is there a blanket prohibition of this option? The sole reason 
provided by the applicants has been reliance on generalised and unsubstantiated 
security concerns. We submit, as discussed below, that the SCA correctly found 
that this does not justify a limitation of the right to further education of all prisoners 
registered for study.  
 
34  Vol 1 Answering Affidavit, para 14.2, p 39. 
35  Vol 6, SCA Judgment, para 8, p 496. 
36  Vol 1, Founding Affidavit, para 21, p 11. 
37  Vol 1, Founding Affidavit, para 23, p 13. 
38  Vol 1, Answering Affidavit, para 20.1, p 42. 
 13 
THE RIGHTS OF PRISONERS 
34. The rights of prisoners must be understood in the context of the primacy of the 
Constitution. Even prior to our constitutional dispensation, prisoners’ rights were 
protected by the residuum principle which traces back through a line of cases.  
35. In Whittaker v Roos and Bateman; Morant v Roos and Bateman, Innes CJ stated: 
“True, the plaintiffs’ freedom had been greatly impaired by the legal 
process of imprisonment; but they were entitled to demand respect for 
what remained. The fact that their liberty had been curtailed could afford 
no excuse for a further illegal encroachment upon it.”39 
 
36. The extent of prisoner’s rights arose in a line of cases concerning prisoners and 
detainees under apartheid legislation. In  Rossouw v Sachs,40 a case concerning 
detention under section 17 of Act 37 of 1963 (the General Laws Amendment Act), 
the then appellate division held that detainees under the Act were not entitled to 
reading and writing materials.41 
37. In the case of Goldberg v Minister of Prisons, a case concerning access to radio 
news and reading material for long-term prisoners, the residuum principle was 
relied on in the minority Judgment of Corbett JA, in holding:  
"It seems to me that fundamentally a convicted and sentenced 
prisoner retains all the basic rights and liberties (using the word in its 
Hohfeldian sense) of an ordinary citizen except those taken away from 
him by law, expressly or by implication, or those necessarily 
inconsistent with the circumstances in which he, as a prisoner, is 
placed. Of course, the inroads which incarceration necessarily make 
upon a person's personal rights and liberties (for sake of brevity I shall 
henceforth speak merely of 'rights') are very considerable. He no 
longer has freedom of movement and has no choice in the place of his 
imprisonment. His contact with the outside world is limited and 
regulated. He must submit to the discipline of prison life and to the 
rules and regulations which prescribe how he must conduct himself 
and how he is to be treated while in prison. Nevertheless, there is a 
 
39  Whittaker v Roos and Bateman; Morant v Roos and Bateman 1912 AD 92 at 122. 
40  Rossouw v Sachs 1964 (2) 551 (A)  
41  Rossouw v Sachs 1964 (2) 551 (A) at 565. 
 14 
substantial residuum of basic rights which he cannot be denied; and, 
if he is denied them, then he is entitled, in my view, to legal redress.”42  
38. The residuum principle was applied by Jansen JA in the case of Mandela v 
Minister of Prisons, holding: 
“On principle a basic right must survive incarceration except insofar as 
it is attenuated by legislation, either expressly or by necessary 
implication, and the necessary consequences of incarceration.”43 
39. In Minister of Justice v Hofmeyr, Hoexter JA held, with reference to the Whittaker 
judgment: 
"The Innes dictum serves to negate the parsimonious and 
misconceived notion that upon his admission to a gaol a prisoner is 
stripped, as it were, of all his personal rights; and that thereafter, and 
for so long as his detention lasts, he is able to assert only those rights 
for which specific provision may be found in the legislation relating to 
prisons, whether in the form of statutes or regulations. . . in truth a 
prisoner retains all his personal rights save those abridged or 
proscribed by law. The root meaning of the Innes dictum is that the 
extent and content of a prisoner’s rights are to be determined by 
reference not only to the relevant legislation, but also be reference to 
his inviolable common law rights."44 
40. These statements are now fortified by the Constitution and the entrenched Bill of 
Rights, which specifically protects the rights of arrested, detained and accused 
persons.45  
41. The residuum principle was first endorsed by this Court in Makwanyane.46 
Chaskalson P emphasised that “prisoners retain all the rights to which every 
person is entitled under [the Bill of Rights] subject only to limitations imposed by 
 
42  Goldberg and Others v Minister of Prisons and Others 1979 (1) SA 14 (A), later referred to with 
approval in Minister of Justice v Hofmeyr 1993 (3) SA 131 (A) 141G-H. 
43  Mandela v Minister of Prisons 1983 (1) SA 938 (A) at 957E-F, cited in Minister of Justice v 
Hofmeyr 1993 (3) SA 131 (A) at para 21. 
44   Minister of Justice v Hofmeyr 1993 (3) SA 131 (A) at 141 C-D. See also Conjwayo v Minister of 
Justice, Legal and Parliamentary Affairs 1992 (2) SA 56 (ZS) at 60J. 
45   See Minister of Correctional Services v Kwakwa 2002 (4) SA 455 (SCA) at para 28, where Navsa 
JA states that the Hofmeyr judgment “has been given fresh impetus by a number of our 
constitutional values such as dignity, equality and humanity.” 
46  S v Makwanyane 1995 (3) SA 391 (CC) at para 142. 
 15 
the prison regime that are justifiable under section 33.”47 
42. The principle recognises that a prisoner retains the constitutional rights of an 
ordinary citizen except those liberty rights (such as freedom of movement) that 
are a necessary consequence of imprisonment.48  
43. As O’Regan J held in her concurring judgment in Makwanyane, “the 
entrenchment of a Bill of Rights, enforceable by a judiciary, is designed, in part, 
to the protect those who are marginalised, the dispossessed and the outcasts of 
our society. They are the test of our commitment to a common humanity and 
cannot be excluded from it.”49 
44. In August v Electoral Commission, this Court said the following in regard to the 
rights of prisoners to vote: 
“They must submit to the discipline of prison life and to the rules and 
regulations which prescribe how they must conduct themselves and 
how they are to be treated while in prison. Nevertheless, there is a 
substantial residue of basic rights which they may not be denied; and 
if they are denied them they are entitled to legal redress.’”50 
 
45. The SCA, in its judgment, acknowledged the importance of the residuum principle 
as established in a line of pre-Constitution cases, but observed that the 
Constitution now confers on prisoners the rights extended to all persons, subject 
to limitation that meets the requirement of section 36 of the Constitution.51  
46. The applicants accept the residuum principle and that incarcerated persons retain 
 
47  Ibid at para 143.  
48  Minister of Correctional Services v Kwakwa 2002 (4) SA 455 (SCA) at para 24.  
49  S v Makwanyane 1995 (3) SA 391 (CC) at para 331.  
50  August and another v Electoral Commission and Others 1999 (3) SA 1 (CC) at para 18. 
51  Vol 6, SCA Judgment, para 16, p 500. 
 16 
their rights, including their socio-economic rights,52 but argue that the impugned 
provision of the Policy does not limit any rights or that, if it does, the limitation is 
justifiable.  
THE POLICY IS UNCONSTITUTIONAL 
47. While the applicants largely focus on the specific facts relating to Mr Ntuli, it is 
well settled that the inquiry into the constitutionality of a law53 is an objective 
inquiry, that must consider the full range of relevant factual circumstances and 
rights affected.54  
48. In finding the prohibition in the Policy to be unconstitutional, the High Court relied 
on the rights to further education and equality. The SCA confirmed the 
unconstitutionality of the Policy primarily on the basis of the right to further 
education.  
49. We submit that the Policy is indeed inconsistent with the Constitution on the basis

Jurisdiction of the High Court to decide the equality challenge 
83. The SCA varied the terms of the High Court’s order insofar as it related to the 
Equality Act, because, according to the SCA, the judge a quo did not have 
jurisdiction to sit as an Equality Court without having been designated.80 The 
applicants had not advanced this argument, which the SCA raised of its own 
accord.  
84. We submit that Matsemela AJ did have the jurisdiction to decide the equality 
challenge. 
85. Section 16(1)(a) of the Equality Act provides that “every Division of the High Court 
 
79  Vol 6, High Court Order, para 1, p 525. 
80  Vol 6, SCA Judgment, paras 13 and 14, p 498. 
 27 
or local seat thereof is an equality court for the area of its jurisdiction.” The SCA’s 
difficulty was not with this, but with the question whether Matsemela AJ -who was 
appointed as an acting judge of the High Court – had been designated as a 
presiding officer in terms of the Equality Act. 
86. It is submitted that the SCA failed to have regard to the amendment of section 16 
of the Equality Act. Previously, section 16(2) required presiding officers to receive 
“social context training”. This had the consequence that only those judges who 
had received the training could be designated as presiding officers under the 
Equality Act. Section 16(2) was deleted by an amendment in 2017.81  
87. In addition, the 2017 amendment added section 16(1)(b), which provides that 
“any judge may be designated in writing by a Judge President as a presiding 
officer of the equality court of the area in respect of which he or she is a judge”.  
88. The position since 2017 is accordingly that any High Court judge may sit as a 
presiding officer of the Equality Court if designated to do so in writing by the Judge 
President of the division.  
89. Before the amendment, several cases which, like the present matter – involved 
reliance on several constitutional rights, including equality – were faced with 
jurisdictional difficulties that resulted in the equality challenge not being 
decided.82 This was inimical to the realisation of the constitutional right to equality.  
90. The 2017 amendment has resolved this difficulty by providing that the Judge 
President may designate any High Court judge to sit as an Equality Court 
presiding officer. It is submitted that, in circumstances where the matter was

91. Put differently, following the statutory amendment that renders all High Court

there is no basis on the papers to find that the High Court was not competent to

appropriate, in a High Court matter in which the right to equality and other rights 
are engaged, for the court to sit as both High Court and Equality Court to enable 
it to decide all the issues on a consolidated basis.83 Matsemela AJ appropriately 
did so.

in circumstances where Mr Ntuli was (at that stage) unrepresented.

belief, culture, language or birth.”  
 
113. When read together, section 18 and regulation 13(4) make clear that reading

material includes material in electronic form.94 Section 13(4) goes on to provide 
grounds under which the Head of a Correctional Centre may prohibit specific 
material may. There is no basis on which study material of the sort with which the 
present application for leave to appeal is concerned would jeopardise safety and 
security or would undermine a person’s sense of personal dignity.  Furthermore, 
the Policy recognises that access to educational materials aids in rehabilitation. 
Personal computers - being used for study purposes - could not therefore be 
removed from cells on the basis that it is “not conducive to rehabilitation” in terms 
of section 18.  
114. Section 38(1)(c) of the Correctional Services Act provides for the individual 
prisoner’s needs in regard to education. A blank approach / policy is contrary to 
the provisions of the Correctional Services Act and the Regulations. The 
approach under the scheme of the Act and Regulations is to recognise and 
guarantee the right of access to reading material (including electronic material), 
subject to specified and defined exceptions. A blanket ban is ultra vires this 
scheme.  
115. Both at common law and in terms of section 39(2) of the Constitution, courts must 
interpret law to promote dignity and the rights contained in the Bill of Rights.  The 
Constitutional Court has specifically applied this interpretive injunction in relation 
to the right to further education in section 29(1)(b) of the Constitution, confirming 
that legislation implicating the right must be interpreted to promote access to 
further education.95

The Policy is irrational 
118. We submit the blanket prohibition on the use of computers for study purposes in 
single cells is not rationally connected to the objective that it seeks to achieve. 
119. It is a fundamental legal principle that the exercise of public power must be 
objectively rational. 
119.1. In Affordable Medicines Trust,97 the Constitutional Court repeated the 
remarks which it had made in the earlier Pharmaceutical Manufacturers 
case:98 
“Rationality in this sense is a minimum threshold requirement 
applicable to the exercise of all public power by members of the 
Executive and other functionaries. Action that fails to pass this 
threshold is inconsistent with the requirements of our Constitution and 
therefore unlawful. The setting of this standard does not mean that the

Courts can or should substitute their opinions as to what is appropriate 
for the opinions of those in whom the power has been vested. As long 
as the purpose sought to be achieved by the exercise of public power 
is within the authority of the functionary, and as long as the 
functionary's decision, viewed objectively, is rational, a Court cannot 
interfere with the decision simply because it disagrees with it or 
considers that the power was exercised inappropriately. A decision 
that is objectively irrational is likely to be made only rarely but, if this 
does occur, a Court has the power to intervene and set aside the 
irrational decision.'”  (Our emphasis) 
 
119.2. In Albutt,99 the Constitutional Court said the following: 
“'The Executive has a wide discretion in selecting the means to 
achieve its constitutionally permissible objectives. Courts may not 
interfere with the means selected simply because they do not like 
them, or because there are other more appropriate means that could 
have been selected. But, where the decision is challenged on the 
grounds of rationality, courts are obliged to examine the means 
selected to determine whether they are rationally related to the 
objective sought to be achieved. What must be stressed is that the 
purpose of the enquiry is to determine not whether there are other 
means that could have been used, but whether the means selected 
are rationally related to the objective sought to be achieved. And if, 
objectively speaking, they are not, they fall short of the standard 
demanded by the Constitution.”  (Our emphasis) 
119.3. In Democratic Alliance v President of the Republic of South Africa,100 the 
Constitutional Court reiterated: 
“The reasoning in these cases shows that rationality review is 
really  concerned with the evaluation of a relationship between means 
and ends: the relationship, connection or link (as it is variously referred 
to) between the means employed to achieve a particular purpose on 
the one hand and the purpose or end itself. The aim of the evaluation 
of the relationship is not to determine whether some means will 
achieve the purpose better than others but only whether the means 
employed are rationally related to the purpose for which the power was 
conferred.” (Our emphasis)

120. In addition to the Constitution and the Correctional Services Act, set out above, 
the Policy must be interpreted in the light of the Higher Education Act 101 of 1997 
and the United Nation’s Standard Minimum Rules for the Treatment of Prisoners 
(“the UN Standards”).101 
120.1. The preamble to the Higher Education Act makes plain that the provision 
of higher education directly implicates constitutional rights and values. It 
declares that for the purposes of regulating Higher Education “it is 
desirable to promote the values which underlie an open and democratic 
society based on human dignity, equality and freedom.” (Our emphasis) 
120.2. The UN Standards recognise the following: 
“Education and recreation 
77. (1) Provision shall be made for the further education of all prisoners 
capable of profiting thereby, including religious instruction in the 
countries where this is possible. The education of illiterates and young 
prisoners shall be compulsory and special attention shall be paid to it 
by the administration. 
 
(2) So far as practicable, the education of prisoners shall be integrated 
with the educational system of the country so that after their release 
they may continue their education without difficulty. 
 
78. Recreational and cultural activities shall be provided in all

Social relations and after-care

121. These standards recognise the benefits or further education, recreational and

cultural activities and maintaining social relations with family. All of these 
combined provide better development opportunities and empower offenders for 
sustainable life after release. However, the blanket prohibition results in 
restricting the use of computers to times which overlap with all recreational and 
cultural activities, and opportunities for family visits and communication. In so 
doing it undermines the standards from which it derives its mandate.  
122. We submit that access to personal computers must be reasonable and rationally 
connected to the purpose of the Policy, which is to enable education and be 
responsive to the needs of the offenders. In the present circumstances the 
prohibition undermines the very education policy it is meant to enable, and the 
Constitutional principles which underpin it. 
Summation  
123. The prohibition must be measured against the rights guaranteed to prisoners 
under the Constitution and the Correctional Services Act and Regulations. The 
Constitution, Act, Regulation, and policy make it clear that inmates have a right 
to pursue their tertiary education and to access the necessary reading and 
educational materials they require. If they meet the requirements for using a 
personal computer then they are allowed to keep a personal computer in the 
correctional facility for educational purposes. The blanket prohibition unlawfully 
infringes these rights by prohibiting access to personal computers to an 
insufficient number of hours.  
124. For all of the reasons canvassed, a blanket prohibition on the use of personal 
computers for study purposes in single cells is unconstitutional and unlawful. It is 
irrational and contravenes the principle of legality, the rights of prisoners to 
 40 
access reading material, their right to access higher education and their rights to 
dignity and equality.

SEPARATION OF POWERS: A COMPETENT ORDER 
145. The applicants contend that the SCA, in setting aside the Policy and prescribing 
how it should be rectified, effectively breached the principle of separation of 
powers and overreached its role under the Constitution.129 They argue that the 
function of deciding whether and what regime should apply in a correctional 
facility is a function which must be left “exclusively” for the applicants to 
determine.130 But this misses a vital check on power.  
146. It is the responsibility of the courts to determine the constitutional validity of any 
law or policy. The applicants do not have unfettered power to implement policies 
which undermine the Constitution.  
147. Section 172(1) of the Constitution requires a court to declare law or conduct that 
is inconsistent with the Constitution invalid, and to make any other order that is 
just and equitable. 
148. This Court has recognised that: 
“In our constitutional democracy, the courts are the ultimate guardians of 
the Constitution. They not only have the right to intervene in order to 
prevent the violation of the Constitution, they also have the duty to do so. 
It is in the performance of this role that courts are more likely to confront 
the question of whether to venture into the domain of other branches of 
government and the extent of such intervention. It is a necessary 
component of the doctrine of separation of powers that courts have a 
constitutional obligation to ensure that the exercise of power by other 
branches of government occurs within constitutional bounds. But even in 
these circumstances, courts must observe the limits of their powers.”131 
 
149. Section 172(1)(a) of the Constitution requires of a court that when deciding a

constitutional matter within its powers, a court must declare that any law or

inconsistency. The SCA found the Policy to be inconsistent with section 29(1)(b) 
of the Constitution and accordingly declared it to be invalid. The SCA thus

150. Section 172(1)(b) further permits a court to make any order that is just and

period and on any conditions, to allow the competent authority to correct the 
defect. The suspension of an order of invalidity may be appropriate having regard 
to the constraints placed on a court in respect of the principles of the separation 
of powers.132 
151. The SCA recognised the limits of its powers when crafting the order, in holding at 
paragraph 35, “There are different ways in which the policy could be formulated 
so as to bring it into conformity with the Constitution, and the appellants should 
be afforded an opportunity to do so.” The applicants were provided twelve months 
within which to reformulate a constitutionally complaint policy and the order of 
invalidity suspended for this period of time. In deference to the separation of 
powers, the SCA did not prescribe the ambit of the Policy. 
152. We submit that in setting aside the Policy and suspending the invalidity for a 
period of 12 months to permit the applicants to redraft the Policy the SCA 
complied with its constitutional mandate and did not undermine the separation of 
powers.

CONCLUSION 
155. The blanket prohibition on incarcerated persons who are registered for academic 
courses from using their personal computers in cells – in every case without 
exception, without regard to the circumstances of the incarcerated person – limits 
the rights to further education, the specific right of incarcerated persons to have 
access to reading material, dignity and equality, and breaches the principle of 
legality.  
156. The applicants have failed to advance a sufficient justification – either in fact or 
in law – for these rights limitations as would satisfy section 36 of the Constitution. 
The high water mark of their justification argument is that cell phones are 
smuggled into correctional facilities. There is no evidence relating to security risks

undertake a second, seeking to educate, develop and rehabilitate himself in 
hopes of a different life after release.  
158. The SCA order strikes an appropriate balance, replacing the blanket prohibition 
of the Policy with a regime that permits individuals registered as students to use 
their personal laptops (without modems and subject to regular inspection) for 
study purposes in their cells. The order permits the applicants to redraft the Policy 
in order to remedy the constitutional defects, and appropriately requires them to 
consult with JICS in doing so.

THE NATURE OF THE PROCEEDINGS  
 
1 This application for leave to appeal concerns the constitutionality of a national 
policy of the Department of Correctional Services: the Correctional Services 
Policy Procedures on Further Education adopted by the Acting National 
Commissioner of Correctional Services in 2007 (“the Policy”). Specifically, the 
portion of the Policy that prohibits, without exception, all incarcerated persons 
who are registered for tertiary academic courses from using personal laptops for 
their studies in prison cells.   
 
 
2 
 
THE ISSUES TO BE DETERMINED 
 
2 It is common cause that inmates, especially maximum offenders, are afforded 
restricted hours outside of their cell.  
3 It is common cause that the Policy prohibits student inmates from using 
computers for study purposes inside their single cells.  
4 The matter raises the following questions: 
4.1 Whether it is in the interests of justice to grant leave to appeal; 
4.2 Whether the prohibition on use of personal computers in single cells for 
study purposes by registered student inmates infringes any, or all, of the 
following constitutional rights: 
(a) further education in terms of section 29 of the Constitution (as 
was found by both the High Court and the SCA) 
(b) conditions of detention consistent with human dignity, 
including access to reading material in terms of section 
35(2)(b);  
(c) human dignity in terms of section 10 of the Constitution 
(d) equality in terms of section 9(3) of the Constitution (as was 
found by the High Court). 
4.3 Furthermore, whether the Policy is inconsistent with the principle of 
legality embedded in the rule of law. 
3 
 
4.4 If so, whether such infringement is justifiable in an open and democratic 
society based on dignity, freedom and equality in terms of section 36 of 
the Constitution. 
4.5 Finally, whether the order crafted by the SCA is just and equitable. 
 
SUMMARY OF THE RESPONDENT’S ARGUMENT  
 
5 The respondent submits that: 
The Policy 
5.1 The Policy is antiquated and does not take into consideration the specific 
study methods of a student inmate in that it fails to accommodate the 
educational needs of students studying with computers.  
5.1.1 For inmates such as Mr Ntuli, a computer is not just the tool for 
studying but is in fact the object of study. It is impossible to study 
through a different medium. 
5.1.2 The SCA recognised that even in courses outside of computer 
sciences, educational materials are most conveniently and 
economically accessed on a computer and course work is now 
routinely composed and submitted electronically. 
5.1.3 Access to computers and electronic study provides benefits to 
students through lower costs and speed of access to materials 
and submissions of assignments. 
5.1.4 Education is an important factor in reducing recidivism in inmates. 
4 
 
5.1.5 The Policy fails to accommodate the educational needs of 
students studying on and with computers. 
The rights of prisoners 
5.2 The rights of prisoners must be understood in the context of the primacy 
of the Constitution. Even prior to our constitutional dispensation, 
prisoners’ rights were protected by the residuum principle. The principle 
recognises that a prisoner retains the constitutional rights of an ordinary 
citizen except those liberty rights (such as freedom of movement) that are 
a necessary consequence of imprisonment.1 The principle was endorsed 
by this Court in cases such as Makwanyane2 and August.3 
5.3 The SCA, in its judgment, acknowledged the importance of the residuum 
principle as established in a line of pre-Constitution cases, but observed 
that the Constitution now confers on prisoners the rights extended to all 
persons, subject to limitation that meets the requirement of section 36 of 
the Constitution. 
Unconstitutionality 
5.4 In finding the prohibition in the Policy to be unconstitutional, the High 
Court relied on the rights to further education and equality. The SCA 
confirmed the unconstitutionality of the Policy primarily on the basis of the 
right to further education. 
 
1 Minister of Correctional Services v Kwakwa 2002 (4) SA 455 (SCA) at para 24.  
2 S v Makwanyane 1995 (3) SA 391 (CC) at paras 142 - 143. 
3 August and another v Electoral Commission and Others 1999 (3) SA 1 (CC) at para 18. 
5 
 
5.5 The Policy is indeed inconsistent with the Constitution on the basis of its 
unjustifiable limitation of the right to further education, in addition to the 
other grounds that Mr Ntuli relied upon in the High Court and the SCA. 
The right to further education 
5.5.1 Further education under section 29(1)(b) includes all forms of 
adult education. It includes tertiary education at a university or 
college. 
5.5.2 The residuum principle applies to prisoners’ right to education 
under section 29 of the Constitution which is infringed by the 
prohibition on the use of computers for study purposes in single 
cells.  
5.5.3 The SCA correctly held that the right is limited by the Policy.  
Access to reading material 
5.5.4 Section 35(2)(e) of the Constitution specifically protects the rights 
of detained persons to have access to reading material. It is also 
provided for in the Correctional Services Act 111 of 1998 (section 
18) and Regulations (13(4)) which expand reading material to 
include material in electronic form. 
The right to dignity 
5.5.5 Dignity requires an individualised approach. Blanket bans which 
restrict rights and which are based on generalised assumptions 
are often constitutionally offensive because they impair dignity. A 
6 
 
general prohibition will, by definition, fail to take account of 
differences between individuals.  
Prohibition on unfair discrimination 
5.5.6 In addition to finding that the policy constitutes an unjustified 
limitation of the right to further education, the High Court held that 
the policy constituted unfair discrimination in terms of the 
Promotion of Equality and Prevention of Unfair Discrimination Act 
4 of 2000 (“Equality Act”) as against the respondent.  
5.5.7 The SCA varied the terms of the High Court’s order insofar as it 
related to the Equality Act, because, according to the SCA, the 
judge a quo did not have jurisdiction to sit as an Equality Court 
without having been designated.4 However, the respondent 
submits that in circumstances where the matter was pleaded as 
an unfair discrimination challenge under the Equality Act, 
appointing Matsemela AJ and allocating the present matter to him 
constituted designation of a qualified judge, in a court with 
jurisdiction, to decide the matter as both High Court and Equality 
Court. 
5.5.8 It is therefore submitted that the Policy is also inconsistent with 
the prohibition on arbitrary application of the law and of unfair 
discrimination in terms of section 6 of the Equality Act or, to the 
 
4  Vol 6, SCA Judgment, paras 13 and 14, p 498. 
7 
 
extent directly applicable, sections 9(1) and (3) of the 
Constitution. 
The principle of legality 
5.5.9 The Policy is inconsistent with the Correctional Services Act and 
Correctional Service Regulations and is not rationally connected 
to the objective is seeks to achieve. On this basis, the Acting 
Commissioner’s adoption of the blanket prohibition on the use of 
personal computers for study purposes in single cells in the Policy 
is ultra vires and irrational and falls to be set aside. 
Limitations analysis 
5.6 The SCA held that the negative freedom that the right to further education 
confers is to restrain the State from taking measures absent justification 
that restrict a person from the right to pursue a course of study. The Policy 
can only limit the manner, times and places where students may access 
personal computers for study purposes to the extent that it is justifiable in 
an open and democratic society based on dignity, freedom and equality. 
The SCA correctly found that the applicants failed to justify the removal 
of personal computers from all prisoners’ single cells, regardless of the 
circumstances and without any discretion or exceptions. 
5.7 The applicants rely on alleged security concerns that are neither 
reasonable nor justifiable. The security concerns arise from the the 
presence of contraband mobile phones. As the SCA concluded, no 
factual basis for any additional risk arising from the granting student-
8 
 
inmates the right to use personal laptops for study purposes in single cells 
was provided on the papers. 
Appropriate relief and separation of powers 
5.8 Section 172(1)(a) of the Constitution requires of a court that when 
deciding a constitutional matter within its powers, a court must declare 
that any law or conduct that is inconsistent with the Constitution is invalid 
to the extent of its inconsistency. The SCA found the Policy to be 
inconsistent with section 29(1)(b) of the Constitution and accordingly 
declared it to be invalid.  
5.9 The applicants were provided twelve months within which to reformulate 
a constitutionally complaint policy and the order of invalidity suspended 
for this period of time. The SCA did not prescribe the ambit of the Policy. 
In setting aside the Policy and suspending the invalidity for a period of 12 
months to permit the applicants to redraft the Policy the SCA complied 
with its constitutional mandate and acted consistently with the separation 
of powers. 
PORTIONS OF THE RECORD THAT, IN THE OPINION OF COUNSEL, ARE 
NECESSARY FOR THE DETERMINATION OF THE MATTER
